summary: Stancil argues that data rarely transforms big, high-leverage decisions; it mostly nudges outcomes at the margins. The real advantage comes from finding small edges that can be repeated frequently, or better yet, using data to discover what you’re already slightly better at and then doing that more often so the edge compounds.
money_quotes: Don’t be intuition-driven or data-driven; be quantity-driven, and look exclusively for places where you can create a durable advantage through frequent, small—and in the aggregate, predictable—bets that you’ve tilted in your favor.
Even small improvements are hard to find.
Don’t use data to get good at something we do a lot; use data to figure out what we’re good at, and then do it more often.
Those advantages come from the boring compounding of small biases.
key_insight: Data’s real power is not in heroic, one-off decisions but in identifying small, repeatable edges and multiplying them through frequency.
